entry.title: Datenvolumen SAP GTS System

Unser System wächst aber stetig - vor allem die Tabelle /SAPSLL/SPLAUD.

Wie kann man das Datenwachstum hier eingrenzen - unsere Vermutung ist das hier Protokolle doppelt und dreifach abgelegt werden und unnötig sind.

Gruß Christoph Greiner

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA TM in Subcontracting Scenario
entry.content:
Dear friends of TM,

Subcontracting, in the form of assembling components to a finished product, is a central part in the logistical chain of many industries.

Assuming a simple subcontracting scenario, the following parties are involved:

  • A customer purchases a certain product
  • A buyer receives the purchase order from the customer. As in this case the product must be assembled from some components, the buyer also acts as a so-called passive subcontractor who provides the necessary components to the active subcontractor who assembles the finished product.
  • The active subcontractor then sends the finished product back to the buyer who delivers it to the customer.

Here are some of the possible scenarios how a subcontracting scenario can be set up between the passive and active subcontractor (or the buyer and the entities which assemble the components) regarding the flow of goods of components and the finished product:

This can further be refined by considering the financial chain when the components are either only handed over to the active subcontractor(s) without changing the ownership (‘Buy – Provide’) or whether the components are ‘sold’ to the active subcontractor (‘Buy – Sell’).

But coming back to the flow of goods, there arise transport requirements which need to be planned and executed. And here, our TM comes into play!

From SAP S/4HANA oP2023 FPS2 on, TM supports in the embedded scenario the outbound and inbound moves of the buyer when he ships and receives from the same plant.

Scenario Description

See at next an exemplary scenario from the view of the passive subcontractor/ buyer with TM being involved.

Step 1 (Purchasing):

At the buyer side a sales order for third party processing is created for the product ‘Mat A’ purchased by the customer. Typically, such sales order items have item type ‘TADS’ which triggers automatically the creation of a respective purchase requisition. From that, a subcontracting purchase order with item type ‘L’ is created. Due to the integration with TM, freight unit 2 for the expected finished product is created. This freight unit 2 is relevant for the later inbound from the subcontractor.

Step 2 (Outbound):

Now, the outbound delivery 1 for the shipment of the components ‘B & C’ to the subcontractor is generated from the subcontracting purchase order e.g. with the Subcontracting Cockpit in transaction ME2ON. Hereby, the components are derived from the bill of material of the finished product. For the transportation planning, the outbound delivery is integrated with TM and for the transport requirements freight unit 1 is created and assigned. The freight unit 1 is now planned for the transport (represented by freight order 1) and the corresponding consignment order 1 is used as basis for the advanced shipping notification (ASN) to the subcontractor with the promised delivery date of the components.

Step 3 (Inbound):

The ASN from the subcontractor with the promised delivery date of the finished product is received and consignment order 2 for inbound is created. Hereby it’s important that the subcontractor received an appropriate reference which he can use in the ASN back to the buyer. Because then, the consignment order 2 finds the corresponding freight unit 2 for inbound (which has meanwhile been planned in freight order 2).

The last step is then the delivery to the customer based on the outbound delivery 2 which is created from the sales order. In this example, the buyer is responsible for at least parts of the transport and the planning happens in freight order 3.

This was a very simple scenario for subcontracting in the sense of assembling components where of course also scheduling agreements could have been used. The other scenarios shown are not yet published for the use with TM. But that does not mean that it’s not possible to use at least parts of them with TM by appropriate system settings.

Benefits of TM

TM as a central part of SAP S/4HANA can add a lot of value for customers using this subcontracting scenario as efficient and reliably planned transports are vital to keep complex logistical chains running.

Some of the key points where TM can improve execution and tracking are:

  • Get full visibility for the transport of components and the finished product including transport relevant dates/times and status (e.g. compliance checks, goods receipt/issue, warehouse handling etc.)
  • All available incoterms are considered for the building of respective stages, covering the responsibilities of risk and cost of the involved parties.
  • Use transport and load planning capabilities of TM and assign capacities for the transports from and to the subcontractor for all possible modes of transport.
  • Send ASN with realistic delivery date (calculated by complex heuristics of TM, including schedules, opening hours etc.) for components to align assembly at active subcontractor
  • Receive ASN with delivery date of finished product from passive subcontractor to align delivery to the customer
  • Support returns processing for finished products triggered by QM notification
  • Ensure freight settlement of all transports from/to the subcontractor
  • Enable tendering and subcontracting of carriers for all transports from/to the subcontractor
  • Trigger dangerous goods check for components
  • Use AI capabilities of TM with use cases ‘Intelligent Goods Receipt’ and ‘Conversational Planning’

See here also at one glance the scenario, some of the components TM can be integrated with, a sample freight unit with its execution tracking and the some of the benefits.

Best regards,

Michael

entry.title: How to Apply CR to only one planning adso in multiprovider?
entry.content:
Hi, experts.

we have an issue with CR on Multiprovider

 

Composite provider CP1 is composed with 3 adso. (as in Query)

adso1 : Actual expense, not plannable

adso2 : Sales,  planable, but not input-ready

adso3 : Expense, planable, input-ready

 

The Characteristic Relationship is applied to adso3 only.

(check, derivation, create method all implemented)

(the number of combination is 1600)

 

When I open query on RSRT, Warning occured : Maximum number of 2,147,483,647 cr exceeded.

After dozens of attempts, I found out that the combination is normally created

only when CR is applied to adso2 as well..(Just Because adso2 is planable...)

adso2 is just for reference.... I don't want to apply CR to adso2....

 

I don't think that N_BASIC_PROV is very helpful...

entry.title: Re: Sending Customer invoice to Multiple recipients in S/...
entry.content:
Hello @SivaPrasad_Vasireddy 

 

Currently, as per the standard configuration, the system sends customer invoices to a single email address.

To enable sending customer invoices to multiple email addresses, you need to implement the APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS BAdI.

For your reference, please find the relevant documentation

Addition of Email Recipients

Best Regards

Vivek Savant

entry.title: Re: Improving latency between SAP BTP and on prem S4 system over RFC connection
entry.content:
Hi Community,

" From documentation : valid values are LAN or WAN (relevant for fast serialization only). In the WAN
case, a slower, but better compression algorithm is used and in addition, the data is analyzed for further
compression options. For LAN a very fast compression algorithm is used, and data analysis is done only
at a very basic level. Hence. the compression ratio is not as good in that case, but in this case the
transfer time is considered not that dominant like for WAN. Choose this value depending on the network
quality between JCo and your target system to optimize the performance. The default setting is LAN. "

 

Thanks for your selfless sharing, it really help us a lot.

🙂

 

Regards,

ZFiori.

entry.title: Re: Does SAP SLT support Azure SQL Database as a source
entry.content:
Unfortunately, answer - NOT supported.

SLT need to create data base triggers on source tables. SLT supports all database types that are supported by SAP, but Azure is not supported DB and as consequence no scripts for Azure

entry.title: Synchronize data between two environments in CCV2
entry.content:
User wants to synchronize data between two SAP Commerce Cloud (CCV2) environments, specifically copying media and database data from production to test environment.

User was advised to use the data backup/restore function for a full copy, with a link to KBA 3308351 for details. For exporting a database to a local development environment, user was advised to involve the db team for a database backup.

Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Synchronize data between two environments in CCV2
entry.content: We would advise to use the data backup/restore function to synchronize data between two SAP Commerce Cloud, see KBA 3308351 - FAQ for Data Backup and Restore - SAP Commerce Cloud (https://me.sap.com/notes/3308351) for details.

entry.title: Best Practices for Managing Users and Groups in Azure and IAS
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Currently, we have the following scenario implemented: We have Azure as the IdP → IAS as a proxy → Cloud Apps. The groups are created in Azure, and users are assigned to those groups in Azure. These groups with their users are then provisioned to SAP IAS. From SAP IAS, the users/groups, such as SAC_Group, are provisioned to the SAC app, or the group ENABLE_Group is provisioned to Enable Now.

Since we, as the SAP team, do not have access to the Azure tenant, I am asking if there is a better approach or any best practices for managing users/groups.

Could we provision only users from Azure and create the groups directly in IAS, then manually assign users to these groups und then provision to Cloud Apps? Are there any disadvantages to this approach?

What are your experiences? How do you handle this in your environment? What are the best practices: Should users/groups be managed in Azure, or should groups be managed directly in IAS?

Which approach is better: Managing users/groups in Azure or provisioning users to IAS and managing the groups there?

Thank you so much!

Best Regards

entry.title: Re: Does SAP SLT support Azure SQL Database as a source
entry.content: Hello, I'm trying to connect SAP SLT to an Azure SQL Database (Azure SQL Server PaaS offering) via DBCO connection. I have configured the firewall and verified that network flows are open for ther required port 1433. For configuring the connection in SLT I used this connection string : OBJEMSSQL_SERVER=<DbServerName>.database.windows.net MSSQL_DBNAME=<DbBame> MSSQL_SE=1 Testing the connection return this error message : sql error 40.532 occured: Cannot open server "xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx" requested by the login. The login failed What is happening is that SAP SLT is resolving FQDN <DbServerName>.database.windows.net to its IP address "xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx", then esatblishing the connection using this IP instead of the FQDN. As a result Azure SQL Server rejects the connection because it can't identify the tenant to which it needs do direct the authentication request. I'd like to know the correct way to configure SLT connection to be able to reach a multi tenant cloud database by its FQDN and not IP address. Or is there any work around for this. Thanks, Madhuri Ingale

entry.title: Re: how can Successfactors data be successfully masked

entry.title: How to Control Module Access in SAP Business One Web Client?
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts! 

In SAP Business One, clients with a professional license have full access to all modules. However, we are looking for a way to configure the system so that only specific modules are accessible based on the client’s actual needs, while restricting companywide access to modules they do not require when using the Web Client.

Can module access be controlled in a way that allows clients to still manage their environment autonomously, while ensuring that these access restrictions cannot be overridden - even if they have a superuser?

I’m really looking forward to hearing your suggestions.

Thank you!

entry.title: IBP Excel Case Sensitivity
entry.content:
Hello

I am experience a strange behaviour in the Excel add-in for IBP. Here is the scenario

A Product description has been created in ECC in mixed case. In this example, the description contained the letters Mg. In subsequent days, the masterdata was updated in ECC to be MG.

Whilst the master data in IBP updated to reflect this, the excel add-in remained as Mg.

However, if I made a significant change to the masterdata (I added a 1 at the beginging) then the fully updated description with MG appeared in excel. 

To make this more mind-blowing, when I removed the 1, the case went back to Mg.

It wouldn't be a huge issue, but for the fact that the case sensitivity means that the planning object cannot be found, and so causes an error in the excel add in when updating a key figure.

Any help appreciated!

entry.title: 全S-IDに対するMFA実装状況の監視に関する問い合わせ
entry.content:
SAPサポートチーム御中

 

 お世話になっております。

 

当社の方針に従い、全てのS-IDに対して多要素認証（MFA）を必須とする予定です。MFAの設定方法は理解していますが、管理者がどのユーザーがMFAを有効にしているかを確認する方法が必要です。

 

既にSAP IDサポートチームに問い合わせを行いましたが、このリクエストに対するサポートは彼らの範囲外であるようです。

 

すべてのユーザーに対するMFAの実装状況を管理者が監視する方法があるかどうか、ご教示いただけますでしょうか。
可能であれば、この情報を一覧で画面上またはダウンロード可能な表形式で閲覧できると理想的です。

 

 ご支援とご理解のほど、よろしくお願い申し上げます。


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: 全S-IDに対するMFA実装状況の監視に関する問い合わせ
entry.content:
ご担当者様

いつもお世話になっております。

全S-IDに対するMFA実装状況について、管理者がユーザーマネージメントアプリケーション画面で確認できます。

具体的な手順には以下の通りです。

1、管理者権限の持つユーザでユーザーマネージメントアプリケーションの「ユーザー」タブにアクセスします。

2、ユーザ一覧にMFAステータス 箇所で確認します。
　✔チェックマークがついているのはすでに有効済みのユーザで、何もついていないのはMFA未有効ユーザです。　

3、画面右上にある”Download List”をクリックすると、CSVファイルとしてローカルで保存し、ローカル上で確認することも可能です。

よろしくお願いいたします。

SAPサポート
Judy

entry.title: BAPI for transaction FB41 posting
entry.content:
Dear all,

I need to create FI Document in program like transaction FB41 In SAP S4 2023.

Requirement:

Posting on account needing VAT Code without VAT Code (FB41 do this).

Last solution would be a batch input but I dislike this...

entry.title: Re: API for Custom fields
entry.content:
Hello,

To integrate the data of added custom fields with an external system in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, you need to enable usage for the relevant APIs in the Custom Fields app. Once you have created the custom fields, follow these steps:

1. Go to the **APIs tab** in the Custom Fields app.
2. Enable usage for the relevant APIs. This ensures that data related to your custom fields can be accessed and integrated with external systems.

You don't necessarily need to create a new API for the custom fields. Instead, you can use the available APIs and configure them to expose the custom fields as required for your integration.

Best regards,

Dequan Xu

entry.title: Re: How Compensation Hierarchies Are Used And Configured For Compensation Planning
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I’m currently working on an implementation of SAP SuccessFactors Compensation and would like to gather your insights and experiences regarding the management of complex relationships using the secondary hierarchy feature.

In our case, we need to account for additional relationships in the compensation process, including:

Distinct final approvers based on employee groups.
Specific tracking roles for certain types of employees.
An extended hierarchy chain involving employees and managers across different legal entities.
I’m looking to understand how the secondary hierarchy in Compensation can be configured to:

Manage these complex relationships without disrupting the standard hierarchy.
Enable flexibility in workflows for salary and bonus reviews.
Ensure data remains performant and maintainable, even with a large user base.
If any of you have faced similar challenges or implemented effective solutions, I would love to hear your thoughts. Any advice on best practices or points of caution would also be greatly appreciated!

Here are the key links for remuneration roles and their descriptions:

Valideur_Final_Rémunération_Classes_Groupe_A

Remuneration link: Final approver for Group A or Class.
Valideur_Final_Rémunération_Groupes_B_C

Remuneration link: Final approver for Groups B or C.
Manager_Rémunération_Classes_Groupe_A

Remuneration link: Submitter for Group A or Class.
Manager_Rémunération_Groupes_B_C

Remuneration link: Submitter for Groups B or C.
RH_Rémunération_Classes_Groupe_A

Remuneration link: HR monitor for Group A or Class.
RH_Rémunération_Groupes_B_C

Remuneration link: HR monitor for Groups B or C.

 

Thank you in advance for your help!

entry.title: Re: Pros and Cons of having one SAP BTP ABAP subaccount c...
entry.content:
Hi Richa_Gupta,

I assume you are talking about exposing your onpremise systems!
If yes, from my perspective, you should consider using at least two subaccounts: one for DEV/TEST systems and another for production systems. I would also use two different Cloud Connector instances, each connected to one of the subaccounts. Exposing multiple backends within a single cloud connector is definitely a valid approach.

Whether this aligns with your company policies and meets your requirements is something I cannot ascertain.

Hope that helps & best regards,
Ingo

entry.title: Migration and upgrade: Understanding Key Approaches
entry.content:
Introduction 

This article outlines potential methodologies for upgrade and migration projects, detailing the responsibilities associated with each phase of the process. 

 The diagram below presents two potential scenarios, both addressing the migration and upgrade from SAP Commerce versions earlier than 2211. While other approaches may theoretically exist, they are restricted by licensing constraints. 

 The following variations assume that the upgrade and migration occur during the cutover phase. This implies that all preparations, bug fixing and DB cleanup have been completed by this stage, and no major issues are anticipated during upgrade or data migration. Additionally, this text outlines specific activities that are directly linked to the SAP Commerce version upgrade and data migration, forming integral components of the overall cutover plan documentation. 

Additionally, SAP Commerce 2211 version must be deployable in SAP Commerce cloud environment. For more information on supported versions, please refer to Supported Releases page and Update Releases page. 

Please note that the following steps for upgrade or migration provide a high-level overview and do not include comprehensive explanations. For in-depth information on the direct and staged approaches, please refer to this article. 

RACI matrix description used in article: 

R = Responsible 

Those who complete the task. There is at least one role with a participation type of responsible, although others can be delegated to assist in the work required.  

A = Accountable (also approver or final approving authority) 

The one ultimately answerable for the correct and thorough completion of the deliverable or task, the one who ensures the prerequisites of the task are met and who delegates the work to those responsible. 

C = Consulted 

Those whose opinions are sought, typically subject-matter experts, and with whom there is two-way communication. 

I = Informed 

Variation #1 

Description 

This approach is regarded as migration and upgrade in a single step, as detailed in this article. It involves upgrading SAP Commerce immediately following the data migration.  

We assume that it is not possible for the customer or CSP to be solely responsible for the upgrade part. This scenario would correspond to variation #2. 

Technical details 

Preparation: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Run pre-migration steps from cutover plan (1) 

R 

CI 

Stop all SAP Commerce production clusters 

R 

CI 

Create production DB copy(2) 

R 

CI 

(1) – This includes tasks specifically related to the on-premise cluster, such as stopping all cron jobs and enabling the maintenance page. Responsibilities for other pre-migration steps should be as detailed in the cutover plan. 

(2) – This refers to a copy of the production database that contains the most recent data changes from the production environment. 

Upgrade and Migration: 

This can be done in a direct or staged approach. 

Direct approach steps: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Prepare the 2211 target build in the cloud portal with DBSync extensions enabled in the localextensions.xml 

I 

R 

Deploy the target 2211 build, selecting the “Initialize database” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Verify the connection to both the source and target databases in the DBSync tab within HAC 

I 

R 

Generate schema diff DDL statements using DBSync in HAC. Review and run required DDL statements 

I 

R 

Execute database migration using DBSync in HAC 

I 

R 

Redeploy the target build, selecting the “Migrate data” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Run smoke tests on migrated 2211 SAP Commerce version 

 R

R 

Bug fixing (bugs related only to migration or upgrade) 

CI 

R 

Staged Approach: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Prepare the 2211 target build in the cloud portal with DBSync extensions enabled in the localextensions.xml 

I 

R 

Adjust system properties for “Application tenant” deployment 

I 

R 

Deploy the target 2211 build, selecting the “Initialize database” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Adjust system properties for “Migration tenant” deployment 

I 

R 

Deploy the target 2211 build, selecting the “Initialize database” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Verify the connection to both the source and target databases in the DBSync tab within HAC 

I 

R 

Generate schema diff DDL statements using DBSync in HAC. Review and run required DDL statements 

I 

R 

Execute database migration using DBSync in HAC 

I 

R 

Adjust system properties for “Application tenant” deployment 

I 

R 

Redeploy the target build, this time selecting the “Migrate data” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Run smoke tests on migrated 2211 SAP Commerce version 

 R

R 

Bug fixing (bugs related only to migration or upgrade) 

CI 

R 

 

Variation #2 

Description 

SAP Commerce 2211 is exclusively a cloud-based release. However, it is permissible to deploy it in an on-premise environment, provided that such deployment does not contravene the licensing agreement. This essentially means that the deployment must be limited to non-production environments. 

It's imperative that no production data interacts with the Hybris system. This can be ensured through proper management of integration configurations and the activation of maintenance pages. Once the upgrade is complete, smoke tests should be conducted to verify the system's functionality. If these smoke tests are successful, the 2211 version can then be migrated from the on-premise environment to SAP Commerce cloud. 

Due to access restrictions to on-premise production systems, SAP cannot assume full responsibility for pre-migration/upgrade steps. Consequently, these steps must be jointly executed. This means SAP will handle upgraded code changes and configuration while deployments and infrastructure work should be managed by Customer. 

Please be aware that this alternative should only be utilized as a fallback when Variation#1 is not feasible for any reason, potentially due to data control requirements from the customer's side. It is important to note that this approach is not advised for dry-run or cutover scenarios. 

There are two possible scenarios regarding responsibilities: 

  1. Customer or CSP is handling the upgrade, while SAP is in charge of the migration 
  2. SAP is responsible for both upgrade and migration

Technical details 

Preparation: 

If customer or CSP is responsible for upgrade: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Run pre-migration steps from cutover plan (1) 

R 

CI 

Stop all SAP Commerce production clusters 

R 

CI 

Create production DB copy(2) 

R 

CI 

 If SAP is responsible for upgrade: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP  

SAP 

Run pre-migration steps from cutover plan (1) 

R 

CI 

Stop all SAP Commerce production clusters 

R 

CI 

Create production DB copy(2) 

R 

CI 

(1) – This includes tasks specifically related to the on-premise cluster, such as stopping all cron jobs and enabling the maintenance page. Responsibilities for other pre-migration steps should be as detailed in the cutover plan. 

(2) – This refers to a copy of the production database that contains the most recent data changes from the production environment. 

Upgrade: 

In this phase, all upgrade steps from the upgrade procedure are executed on the on-premise system, where the SAP Commerce version is upgraded from 2205 to 2211. 

If customer or CSP is responsible for upgrade: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Execute upgrade steps from upgrade procedure to upgrade SAP Commerce version to 2211 on on-premise environment(1) 

R 

CI 

Run smoke tests on 2211 SAP Commerce version (1) 

R 

CI 

Bug fixing (bugs related to upgrade) 

R 

CI 

(1) – SAP Commerce is running on non-productive on-premise environment using a production-synced DB copy (copy of the production database that contains the most recent data changes from the production environment)

Please note that the aforementioned responsibilities lie with the customer. This also includes bug fixing prior to the cutover phase. However, during the cutover phase, if any bug fixing is necessary after the upgrade, it is recommended to share responsibility in order to minimize the overall migration and upgrade timeline. 

If SAP is responsible for upgrade: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP/SAP 

SAP 

Execute upgrade steps from upgrade procedure to upgrade SAP Commerce version to 2211 on on-premise environment (1) 

R 

CI 

Run smoke tests on 2211 SAP Commerce version (1) 

CI

R 

Bug fixing (bugs related to upgrade) 

CI 

R 

(1) – SAP Commerce is running on non-productive on-premise environment using a production-synced DB copy (copy of the production database that contains the most recent data changes from the production environment)

Migration: 

Next step after SAP Commerce is upgraded and tested on on-premise environment is to execute migration to SAP Commerce cloud. This can be done in a direct or staged approach after smoke tests are successfully executed on on-premise. 

Direct approach steps: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Prepare the 2211 target build in the cloud portal with DBSync extensions enabled in the localextensions.xml 

I 

R 

Deploy the target 2211 build, selecting the “Initialize database” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Verify the connection to both the source and target databases in the DBSync tab within HAC 

I 

R 

Generate schema diff DDL statements. Review and run if required additional DDL statements 

I 

R 

Execute database migration using DBSync in HAC 

I 

R 

Redeploy the target build, selecting the “No migration required” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Run smoke tests on 2211 SAP Commerce version 

R 

R 

Bug fixing (bugs related to migration only) 

CI 

R 

Staged Approach: 

 

Action name 

Customer/CSP 

SAP 

Prepare the 2211 target build in the cloud portal with DBSync extensions enabled in the localextensions.xml 

I 

R 

Adjust system properties for “Application tenant” deployment 

I 

R 

Deploy the target 2211 build, selecting the “Initialize database” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Adjust system properties for “Migration tenant” deployment 

I 

R 

Deploy the target 2211 build, selecting the “Initialize database” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Verify the connection to both the source and target databases in the DBSync tab within HAC 

I 

R 

Generate schema diff DDL statements using DBSync in HAC. Review and run, if required, DDL statements 

I 

R 

Execute database migration using DBSync in HAC 

I 

R 

Adjust system properties for “Application tenant” deployment 

I 

R 

Redeploy the target build, this time selecting the “No migration required” option, to the target production environment 

I 

R 

Run smoke tests on 2211 SAP Commerce version 

R 

R 

Bug fixing (bugs related to migration only) 

CI 

R

entry.title: Creating a Custom Bank Data Model in SAP MDG (Reuse)
entry.content:
Background

This blog explains how to create a custom data model in SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) with reuse mode. The new data model handles bank master data. The idea of this approach is manually create, update or delete bank master data in the BNKA table within the MDG governance process.

Introduction

Sap MDG provides a robust framework for managing master data. However in some cases, the standard data models may not fully meet your organization’s requirements. This is where
creating a custom data model comes in handy. İn this blog, we will go through the step-by-step process of creating a custom bank data model in sap MDG.

Steps for Creating the Custom Bank Data Model

Step 1: Define the Custom Bank Data Model

  • Navigate to the Data Model Screen:
    To start, you need to create a new data model via transaction MDGIMG under General Settings-> DataModeling->Edit DataModel.


Figure 1 – Edit Data Model

  • Create a Custom Data Model:
    General Settings-> Data Modeling -> Edit Data Model-> Click on New Entries-> Enter data model name and Description and save


Figure 2 – Create Data Model

  • Create a Custom Attributes:
    If required you can entities like Bank with additional attributes, such as bank category or bank type.


Figure 3 – Create Attributes

Step 2: Define the Change Request Type

Nex, you need to define the change request type that will be used for creating and managing the bank data in this new model.

  • Define Change Request Type:
    In MDGIMG, navigate to General Settings->Process Modeling->Change Requests-> Create Change Request Type


Figure 4 – Create Change Request Type

Create a new change request type specifically for your custom model (e.g., ZBNK01).
Assign the custom data model you created to this change request type, ensure that the correct workflow and approval process are configured for this change request type.

Step 3: Configure the Fiori Catalog and Group

To make your custom model accessible in Fiori configure a catalog and group.

  • Create Fiori Catalog and Group:
    In the Fiori Launchpad Designer, create a catalog (e.g., zbnkidct ) and create a group (e.g., zbnkidgrp).


Figure 5 – Create Fiori Catalog

Step 4: Create a Custom UI in SAP Fiori

Navigate to General Settings->UI Modeling->Manage UI Configurations.

A custom UI in sap fiori will enhance the user experience by providing a structured interface for interacting with the data model.


Figure 6 – Create Custom UI

Testing

Once the configuration is complete, perform testing to ensure the data model and UI are functioning as expected.


Figure 7 – Search Result


Figure 8 – Create new bank entry


Figure 9 – BNKA table result

Conclusion

With fiori integration, workflow management field-level, you can ensure a streamlined, user-friendly experience.

Testing ensures all configurations are working as expected.enhancing data quaity and operational efficiency. If you have questions or tips to share, feel free to leave a comment below!

Thank you for reading,
Best Regards.

Ahmet

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Functionality for EDI Processing: Inbound Order Creation by API vs. IDOC (Part 1)
entry.content:
In my previous post, I described how to select an API for creating orders based on EDI data and how to test a payload using SE80. This article focuses on a key aspect of EDI integration: mapping external partners to internal partners and determining the sales area. The mapping of external material numbers to internal ones will be covered in the next part. My goal is to compare whether the API supports the features available for IDocs, identify any new opportunities, and highlight deviations that require consideration.

The purpose of this analysis is to evaluate whether the selected API can replace IDoc-based processing. To achieve this, I examine the logic that aligns closely with classical IDocs. While the API may offer extended functionality, such cases are beyond the scope of this article.

As mentioned in my previous post, the appropriate API for inbound order creation is ORDERREQUEST_IN: Sales Order/Customer Return - Create, Update, Cancel (B2B). This API is compared with the IDoc ORDERS processing, which uses process code ORDE and function module IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS.

TLDR: Main Comparison Results

The B2B API supports most of the IDoc logic in the described areas while introducing several useful new features:

  • The B2B API ORDERREQUEST_IN is not a wrapper for IDoc processing but a separate implementation.
  • Partner determination uses EDPAR, similar to IDoc processing.
  • New feature: Partner determination by GLN, based on partner master data.
  • Order type and sales area determination use EDSDC. Simplified logic is recommended, though legacy logic is also supported.
  • Material determination employs similar logic for vendor material and customer material info records.
  • New feature: Material and UoM determination by EAN/GTIN, based on material master data.
  • Legacy logic for EAN/GTIN based on VB12 is not supported, and additional cross-checks have been introduced in the determination logic.

For a detailed explanation of partner and sales area determination, refer to the following sections. This article is extensive, so the detailed description of material determination will be published in a subsequent post.

 

API ORDERREQUEST_IN: Technical Details

I could not find any clear and detailed documentation of the business logic for this API. Therefore, most of the information was derived through testing and analyzing the code. From a technical perspective, the ORDERREQUEST_IN API is not a wrapper for IDoc-related code; it is implemented as separate logic.

My investigation focused on the methods within the LOG_EDI_SD_IMPL_ORDER package. The logic is distributed across multiple classes, and in some cases, the comments within the methods provide valuable insights. For example, as shown below, the MAP_PRODUCT method in the CL_EDI_SD_ORDR_MAPPING class contains a sufficient description of its logic.

MAP_PRODUCT: material mapping logic for EDI in SD.

Partner Determination Logic

For illustration, I will use the same organizational model example as in the article about Partner Determination for Inbound Orders .

Your company is a supplier, and you have the following customer:

ACME Group is a large multi-division company operating across various markets. They leverage economies of scale by using a group entity to manage their EDI operations. In your market, they are represented by the legal entity ACME Corp, and you ship goods to their distribution centre, ACME DC.

ACME sends sales orders through their EDI provider, which converts the messages and forwards them to your EDI provider. Your provider then translates these messages into API requests for processing in your SAP system. As it is traditional for Europe, ACME uses GLNs (Global Location Numbers) for communication and business parties. Additional details about their GLNs are as follows:

Master Records in the Supplier's SAP ERP

  • ACME Group: GLN 7089900000009 - 246
  • ACME Corp: GLN 7089900000016 - 211
  • ACME DC: GLN 7089900000023 - 212

EDI flow: buyer to seller via providers

To create a sales order in the SAP system, external partners must be mapped to SAP master data as follows:

  • Sender → SAP Group Customer
  • Buyer → SAP Sold-to Party
  • Delivery Point → SAP Ship-to Party
  • Supplier → SAP Sales Organization and Distribution Channel

Partner Determination: Sender

The sender partner is specified in the  <MessageHeader> section, outside of the Order segments. I consider it a communication partner, as opposed to business partners like Sold-to and Ship-to.

Example of XML structure:

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
<MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
    <InternalID>246</InternalID>
    <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
</MessageHeader>

 

Based on the XML structure, two types of IDs can be used for sender determination:

  • InternalID
  • StandardID

The InternalID is the preferred value and will be processed regardless of the presence of the StandardID. Technically, it can be any dummy value, which will be visible in AIF monitoring. As mentioned in my previous article, any value is acceptable if business partners are provided as internal SAP numbers. However, this value is critical for EDPAR determination. In the EDPAR table, there is a validation to ensure that the partner number exists as a business partner. As a result, it is recommended to use a relevant partner number. In my example, I use customer 246, which represents an SAP group customer.

If the InternalID is not provided, the StandardID will be processed instead. According to comments in SAP program code, a GLN number is expected here (not a DUNS number, which would be useful for the US market).

SAP code includes the following comment:

 

*---> Only 1 standard ID should be sent (GLN) - DUNS is not supported
*---> If there is one, we assume it is a GLN

 

Partner determination is based on the GLN from customer master data. If a unique partner exists with the provided GLN, the corresponding SAP partner number will be determined and processed further. Otherwise, processing will fail.

In comparison, for IDOC processing, sender determination requires the setup of partner profiles using transaction code WE20 (Partner Profiles). These can be configured as either KU (Customer) or LS (Logical System), with different logic applied in the associated mappings. However, in the case of IDOC, an intermediate entity like the WE20 Partner Profile is not required.

Sender Partner Error Handling

Any value is applicable for InternalID, but it must match the EDPAR settings if these are in use. If the internal sender ID is incorrect and does not match the EDPAR settings, an error will occur during business partner determination. Business partner determination, which operates based on SAP internal numbers or the GLN from master data, is not affected by the sender ID.

If StandardID is used and the sender partner cannot be determined from the provided GLN (e.g., if it is missing or not unique), the error message is self-explanatory but does not specify the partner type in question.

GLN error: Unable to uniquely determine business partner in SAP EDI.

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
  <standard>
    <faultText> Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
    <faultDetail>
      <severity>error</severity>
      <text>Business partner cannot be uniquely determined by GLN 7089900000099</text>
      <id>013(EDI_SD)</id>
    </faultDetail>
  </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

Partner Determination: Business Partners

Business partner information is stored in the Order/Party segment, and the basic determination logic is described in the code as follows:

 

*---> There are 3 possible ways to determine the party internal ID:
*1) If the supplier Party ID is sent, it is taken as the internal party ID 
*   - If incorrect → error
*2) If the buyer Party ID is sent, it is used to determine the internal party *ID 
*   - If incorrect → error
*3) If the global location number (GLN) is sent, it is used to determine the *internal party ID 
*   - GLN is retrieved from the BP master record

 

Similar to IDOC processing, the internal SAP number is the first option, followed by the external ID, which is determined using the EDPAR table mapping. However, unlike IDOC processing, an additional GLN step is included, leveraging the partner master data. Based on my experience, it was the most desired option to use GLN from customer master data, without any mapping tables!

For IDOC processing, my recommendation is to use the GLN as an external number and map it to SAP numbers via the EDPAR table (transaction VOE4) or, if the GLN is not provided, use the external partner number. For this API logic I see three reasonable options to consider:

  • GLN from master data (GlobalLocationNumber field) if the master data quality is sufficient for unique determination.
  • GLN as an external number, with additional maintenance effort but ensuring robust and separate mapping. In this case, the GLN must be treated as the BuyerPartyID.
  • External number (GlobalLocationNumber) if the GLN is not provided.

According to the code, sequential determination is not supported. For example, if the BuyerPartyID is provided but determination fails, the logic does not attempt to use the GLN, even if it is available.

While mapping the BuyerPartyID to SAP numbers uses the same EDPAR table, SAP provides a dedicated section in SPRO for SOAP API-related settings:
SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Electronic Data Interchange > EDI Messages > Convert External to Internal Partner Numbers.
I do not see any functional difference between using this SPRO option and the VOE4 (Sales EDI Conversion) transaction.

IMG configuration: External-to-internal partner number mapping in SAP EDI.

Let’s explore Business Partner Determination using examples of Sold-to and Ship-to determination with my test payloads.

Business Partner Determination by external partner numbers

The Party segment contains the Party type qualifier (PartyType) and the External Partner ID (BuyerPartyID). The GLN (Global Location Number) may also be provided in the GlobalLocationNumber field; however, if the BuyerPartyID is provided, the GLN will always be ignored. The qualifiers SoldTo and ShipTo are self-explanatory.

Here is a part of a test payload example:

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_201124_3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
    <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
    <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
    <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
    <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
</Order>

 

To process such payloads, the following EDPAR settings are required:

EDPAR table: Mapping external partners to internal numbers in SAP.

In this case, 246 is the sender number, provided directly in SenderParty > InternalID or determined by the GLN specified in SenderParty > StandardID. The logic functions similarly to the Customer as Sender logic used for IDOCs.

I am testing this using SE80, as explained in my previous article.

API test request: XML structure for EDI order creation in SAP.

Order request example:

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-23T02:58:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
<InternalID>246</InternalID>
   <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_01</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-22</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header Note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material Sales Text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

 

 

The result of processing can be found in /AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling. The sender partner number (246) and the customer PO number are used as nodes in the tree structure.

EDI error log: Key messages for inbound sales order processing.

The sales order created by this request is:

Standard Order: Overview of sales order created via EDI.

Business Partner Determination by GLNs

In this second example, I will demonstrate how the determination logic works based on GLN numbers stored in the partner master record (General Data > Identification).

Business Partner: Identification with GLN and check digit in SAP.

In the payload example below, the Sender, Sold-to, and Ship-to are determined by their respective GLNs:

API request XML: EDI order details for testing in SAP.

Order request example, GLN partners

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-22T03:58:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_01_GLN</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-22</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header Note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material Sales Text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

Created sales order:

Standard Order: Overview of sales order created via EDI (GLN based determination)

Business Partner Determination: Error Handling

If an error occurs during partner determination, the fault description includes an explanation of which step failed.

For example, if partner determination fails during the step based on BuyerPartyID, the response will be as follows:

Error: Sold-to party role not determined in SAP EDI response.

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global"                        xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined</text>
            <id>003(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

If only the GlobalLocationNumber is provided and the business partner cannot be determined from the given GLN (e.g., if it is missing or not unique), the error message is self-explanatory but does not specify the partner type in question.

Error response: GLN ambiguity prevents business partner determination in EDI.

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" 
                         xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Business partner cannot be uniquely determined by GLN 7089900000096</text>
            <id>013(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

Order Type and Sales Area Determination Logic

When creating a sales order in the SAP system, the order type and sales organization data must be determined. SAP developers aimed to implement reasonable logic while considering legacy logic previously used, such as for IDOC processing. Some insights and reflections on this can be found in the program code comments.

Although sales area data assignment uses the same EDSDC table, SAP provides a dedicated section in SPRO for SOAP API-related settings:
SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Electronic Data Interchange > EDI Messages > Assign Customer/Supplier to Sales Organization Data.

I do not see any significant differences between using the SPRO option and transaction VOE2 (Sales EDI Customer/Supplier).

IMG: Assign customer/supplier to sales organization data in SAP EDI.

Several options are provided for determination logic. Let’s review the following option:

Order Type and Sales area determination by BuyerPartyID of Supplier Party 

According to the code comments, the recommended approach is to assign the Sold-to SAP customer number and the Supplier Party BuyerPartyID to the required order type and sales area.

 

*---> In the new standard case the BuyerPartyID of the Supplier Party (LF) is used to determine the sales area and document type.
*     This is the prefered option from now on.

 

Payload example (Sold-to is determined by EDPAR table):

 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_201124_3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>

 

 

  • The EDPAR table is used to map the BuyerPartyID to the SAP customer number. This record must be created for Sold-to determination.

EDPAR table: Sold-to party mapping from external to internal number.

  • The EDSDC table is used to assign the order type and sales area to the SAP customer number of the Sold-to party and the BuyerPartyID for the Supplier party.

EDSDC table: Customer-to-vendor assignment with sales organization details.

Order Type and Sales Area Determination by BuyerPartyID of Sold-to Party 

According to the code comments, this option is not recommended but is supported for compatibility purposes.

In this case, the Supplier Party section is not required. The order type and sales area are assigned to the Sold-to SAP customer number and the external number of the same Sold-to party.

Payload example: 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_2</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
    <OrderItem>

 

Required Settings:

  • The EDPAR table is used to map the BuyerPartyID to the SAP customer number. This record must be created for Sold-to determination.

EDPAR table: External-to-internal number mapping for sold-to party.

  • The EDSDC table is used to assign the order type and sales area to the SAP customer number of the Sold-to party and the BuyerPartyID for the same Sold-to party.

EDSDC table: Mapping customer to vendor with sales org and division details.

Order type and Sales area Determination: Error handling

If no record matching the inbound parameters is found, the following response is received:

Error: Sales area cannot be determined in SAP EDI response.

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" 
                         xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Sales area cannot be determined</text>
            <id>001(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

Acknowledgements and System Overview

Thanks to my colleagues at Capgemini and others who explored this topic with me across numerous projects. Also, thanks to Capgemini for providing me with a sandbox system, which I used for investigations, creating test examples, and preparing screenshots. The sandbox was SAP S/4HANA 2022 (S/4HANA On-Premise Release 2022 SP 01, February 2023 FPS).

entry.title: Time accounts
entry.content:
Hello,

There is a requirement which must be following rolling period concept. There are around 5 leave types which are under rolling period which will have cap of 480 hrs. Along with that each individual leave types have their own max number of hours.

Below is the example of leave type

Leave Type

Policy explanation

A

1. Max 48hrs per application

2. should be based on rolling period

3. When leave applied then it should get adjusted in Annual Leave and Sick Leave. Along with that it should be adjusted against 480hrs.

B

1. max 480hrs in total

2. Should follow the rolling period concept

C

1. max 160hrs per application

2. there should be two picklists, where one picklist with yes or no to agree the policy and the second picklist is sub leave type. 

3. Second picklist value to consider as 1,2,3

D

1. Similar to Leave type C where it will be 2 picklists.

2. One with paid or unpaid as options. Where paid will have 64hrs application max and unpaid will have max of 16hrs per application

E

1. Max 560hrs to be available which is beyond the mentioned cap hours

2. The limit for this leave is beyond the cap 480 hrs. However, it will run concurrently with the cap hours.

 

Below is the example for the Rolling period policy

R.P Start Date

3-Mar-25

R.P End Date

2-Mar-26

Max hrs is 480 including all leave types which has the rolling period concept

 

Rolling Year Leave types

Start Date

End Date

Calendar days

Hours

Calculation during rolling year
** Thumb Rule : Maximum of 12 Weeks/672 hours = FMLA Unpaid in a rolling year

Remarks

A

3-Mar-25

8-Mar-25

6

48

432

System should allow employee to apply leave as it is firs leave application and the rolling period starts from 03-March-2025, paralley Annual Leave and SL wll be deducted and if there is no sufficient balance combining both PTO and SL it should throw error


B

1-Apr-25

20-Apr-25

20

160

272

System should allow employee to apply leave, as this is within rolling period, no PTO accrual happens

D unpaid

30-Aug-26

31-Aug-26

10

16

No hours reduces in the current rolling period

System should consider the current leave appliaction dates as the another rolling period as the current rolling period ends on 02-March-2026.

C

12-Dec-25

16-Dec-25

5

40

232

System should allow employee to apply leave, as this is within rolling period,  ALaccrual happens

D paid

14-Jan-26

15-Jan-26

2

16

216

System should allow employee to apply leave, as this is within rolling period, ALaccrual happens

C

18-Dec-25

22-Dec-25

5

40

192

System should allow employee to apply leave, as this is within rolling period,AL accrual happens

D unpaid

14-Feb-26

15-Feb-26

10

16

176

System should allow employee to apply leave, as this is within rolling period, AL accrual happens

E

1-Mar-26

9-May-26

70

560

-344

1. In this Scenario, even if employee do not have enough  availability for the rolling year but since its 'E Leave', system will allow and admin team will take care of this application from Backend.

2. Another scenario can be, where E Leave is the first application in terms of rolling year then system should not allow any further A /B/C/D for that respective rolling year.

Based on the above scenario, when the last leave ‘E’ is applied the end date is beyond the End date of rolling period. In this scenario new rolling period will be started from 3rd-March-2026 again until 02-March-2027

entry.title: Stock Determination Process in MM
entry.content:
To outline the stock determination strategy based on quantity in the storage location, allowing for customization according to specific requirements.

Stock determination in SAP Material Management (MM) is a crucial process that helps in deciding from which storage location materials should be withdrawn for goods issues and stock transfers. This process ensures efficient inventory management and supports various business processes by implementing predefined strategies.

The stock determination strategy is based on the quantity of materials available in different storage locations.

Step 1: Define Stock Determination Strategies (Transaction: OSPX)

Spro > Materials Management > Inventory Management and Physical Inventory > Stock Determination >Define Strategies for Stock Determination.

Define new entries for stock determination group

Navigate to Determination rule

click new entries and create a rule 

Navigate to Determination Header Table

Select line item and navigate to Details

Strategy defined based on the Quantity basis from low stock levels to High stock levels

Step 2: Assign Stock Determination Rule to Application (Transaction: BFMM)

Spro > Materials Management > Inventory Management and PhysicalInventory > Stock Determination > Assign Stock Determination Rule in the Applications >  Inventory Management

Assign the rule to respective movement type as per your requirement

Step 3 : Assign Stock Determination group in the Material Master

Step 4: Stock Overview (Transaction: MMBE)

Step 5: Goods Issue (Transaction: MIGO)

As per our stock determination strategy, Storage locations auto populated under stock selection tab (Less stock levels to High levels)

(Even you can drill down the selection strategy by maintaining the relevant indicators/data in Stock determination header table)

Click copy and post the document.

Step 5: Display Material Document
